blocks.text: If z is a function of x and y, i.e., z = f(x, y), the total differential dz is obtained from the partial differentials dx and dy by the use of the following formula:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: The total differential of a function z in terms of its partial derivatives with respect to x and y.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of calculus, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like z, x, and y.

blocks.caption: Figure 21. The change in z due to the change in x and y.
blocks.description: This figure illustrates how a point on a surface changes as it moves in the x-y plane, reflecting the total differential of z with respect to x and y.

blocks.latex: \text{or total change in } z = \Delta z = \text{change in } z \text{ due to the change in } x.
blocks.description: Defines the total change in z as a function of the changes in x and y.

blocks.text: Let P be a point (x, y) on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near P on the surface. The change of position is almost like a line segment from P to Q on the surface (y being kept constant).
